questions.question: Art. 38.3.1° of the Irish Constitution provides for Special Courts in which circumstances?

options.A: Whenever the Oireachtas considers it expedient to remove a case from the ordinary courts
options.B: When the ordinary courts are 'inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice, and the preservation of public peace and order' — a determination made by law
options.C: Only during a declared state of emergency under Art. 28.3
options.D: Only for offences carrying a maximum sentence of life imprisonment

questions.explanation: Art. 38.3.1° provides that 'special courts may be established by law for the trial of offences in cases where it may be determined in accordance with such law that the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice, and the preservation of public peace and order.' The key constitutional trigger is the 'inadequacy' of the ordinary courts — not mere expediency or the seriousness of the offence. The determination of inadequacy is made 'in accordance with such law' — in practice, by the Director of Public Prosecutions under the Offences Against the State Act 1939.

questions.question: The Special Criminal Court (SCC) was established under which legislation, and what is its current composition?

options.A: The Criminal Justice Act 1984 — composed of three Circuit Court judges
options.B: The Offences Against the State Act 1939 — composed of three judges drawn from the District Court, Circuit Court, High Court, or a practising barrister, sitting without a jury
options.C: The Criminal Law Act 1976 — composed of one High Court judge and two lay assessors
options.D: The Courts (Establishment and Constitution) Act 1961 — composed of five High Court judges

questions.explanation: The Special Criminal Court was established under the Offences Against the State Act 1939 (as amended). It sits without a jury and is composed of three judges drawn from the judiciary (District, Circuit, or High Court) or qualified practitioners. The court was first established in 1972 and deals primarily with scheduled offences under the Offences Against the State Act (including certain terrorist and organised crime offences) and cases where the DPP certifies that the ordinary courts are inadequate. Its decisions are reached by majority vote.

questions.question: The DPP's power to send a case to the Special Criminal Court by certificate is contained in s.47(2) of the Offences Against the State Act 1939. The constitutional validity of this power has been upheld on the basis that:

options.A: The DPP's decision is unreviewable by any court as a matter of prosecutorial discretion
options.B: The Oireachtas may validly empower the DPP to make such a determination, as Art. 38.3 expressly contemplates that the determination of inadequacy be made 'in accordance with such law' — provided the decision is not made in bad faith or for improper purposes
options.C: The DPP's certificate creates an irrebuttable presumption that the ordinary courts are inadequate
options.D: The Art. 38.5 jury trial guarantee does not apply to cases sent to the Special Criminal Court

questions.explanation: Art. 38.3 expressly provides that the determination of ordinary courts' inadequacy is to be made 'in accordance with such law' — meaning the Oireachtas may validly delegate this determination to an appropriate authority. The Supreme Court in Kavanagh v Ireland [1997] 1 IR 321 confirmed that the DPP's certificate power under s.47(2) is constitutionally valid as an exercise of the authority permitted by Art. 38.3. However, the DPP's decision, while not subject to merits review (the courts will not second-guess the DPP's assessment), may be reviewed for bad faith, improper motive, or constitutional invalidity.

questions.question: Can an accused person challenge the DPP's decision to send their case to the Special Criminal Court by certificate?

options.A: No — the DPP's decision is absolutely unreviewable as part of prosecutorial discretion
options.B: Yes — by judicial review in the High Court, but only on limited grounds: bad faith, improper motive, or a failure to consider relevant factors; the courts will not review the merits of the DPP's assessment that ordinary courts are inadequate
options.C: Yes — by application directly to the Special Criminal Court itself, which may remit the case to the Circuit Court
options.D: No — once the DPP certifies, the accused waives all right to challenge the forum

questions.explanation: Kavanagh v Ireland [1997] 1 IR 321 (Supreme Court) confirmed that while the courts will not review the merits of the DPP's certificate (i.e., will not decide whether the ordinary courts were in fact inadequate), the decision is not absolutely unreviewable. Judicial review is available on the limited grounds of bad faith, manifest improper motive, or a failure to consider relevant matters. The courts afford a wide margin of discretion to the DPP on this essentially security-related assessment but retain supervisory jurisdiction to prevent its abuse.

questions.question: The right to trial by jury (Art. 38.5) does not apply to Special Criminal Court proceedings because:

options.A: Art. 38.5 is disapplied during national emergencies
options.B: Art. 38.3 creates a constitutional exception to the jury trial guarantee in Art. 38.5 — where cases are properly sent to the Special Criminal Court under Art. 38.3, the jury trial right is displaced by the express constitutional provision for special courts
options.C: The SCC only deals with minor offences to which Art. 38.5 never applies
options.D: The accused is deemed to have waived jury trial by virtue of the DPP's certificate

questions.explanation: Art. 38.5 guarantees trial by jury for non-minor offences, but this guarantee is expressly qualified by Art. 38.2 (minor offences) and Art. 38.3 (special courts). The constitutional architecture deliberately provides for exceptions to jury trial, one of which is the Special Criminal Court established under Art. 38.3. Where a case is validly sent to the SCC in accordance with Art. 38.3 and the enabling legislation, the Art. 38.5 guarantee is constitutionally displaced. This does not mean fair trial rights are waived — Art. 38.1 (due course of law) continues to apply in SCC proceedings.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the application of Art. 38.1 (due course of law) to Special Criminal Court proceedings?

options.A: Art. 38.1 does not apply to the SCC — the Art. 38.3 exception removes all fair trial guarantees
options.B: Art. 38.1 continues to apply fully to SCC proceedings — the accused retains all fair trial rights including disclosure, legal representation, the presumption of innocence, and the right to call and cross-examine witnesses; only the jury trial requirement is displaced
options.C: Art. 38.1 applies to SCC proceedings only in so far as not inconsistent with the emergency powers of the Oireachtas
options.D: Art. 38.1 applies only to the sentencing phase of SCC proceedings, not to the trial itself

questions.explanation: Art. 38.3 creates an exception to the jury trial requirement but does not displace the broader constitutional guarantee of fair trial under Art. 38.1. This distinction is critical: in SCC proceedings, the accused retains all constitutional fair trial rights — the right to disclosure, to legal representation, to cross-examine witnesses, to the presumption of innocence, and to have all elements of the offence proven beyond reasonable doubt. The special feature of the SCC is that these rights are exercised before a three-judge court without a jury, not that they are abrogated.

questions.question: In Kavanagh v Governor of Mountjoy Prison [2002] 3 IR 97 (Supreme Court), the court addressed the constitutional question of whether detention on remand pending SCC trial violated the accused's constitutional rights. The case is also important because it confirmed:

options.A: That persons remanded in custody to the SCC have no constitutional rights
options.B: That Art. 38.3 is a constitutionally valid derogation from jury trial rights, and the SCC's existence and jurisdiction are constitutionally sound where the DPP's certificate power is properly exercised
options.C: That the SCC must be abolished once an emergency has passed
options.D: That SCC proceedings violate Art. 6 ECHR as interpreted by the European Court of Human Rights

questions.explanation: Kavanagh v Governor of Mountjoy Prison [2002] 3 IR 97 reaffirmed the constitutional validity of Art. 38.3 and the SCC framework. The Supreme Court confirmed that Art. 38.3 provides a constitutionally authorised exception to jury trial, that the DPP's certificate power under s.47(2) Offences Against the State Act 1939 is constitutionally valid (subject to limited review for bad faith), and that an accused's detention pending SCC trial does not in itself violate constitutional rights provided the framework is properly engaged. The case is part of the established line of authority upholding the constitutional legitimacy of the SCC.

questions.question: Which category of offences automatically fall within the SCC's jurisdiction as 'scheduled offences' under the Offences Against the State Act 1939?

options.B: Offences under the Offences Against the State Act itself (e.g., membership of an unlawful organisation, possession of firearms with intent to endanger life) and other offences scheduled by Government order
options.D: Any offence where the accused has a previous conviction

questions.explanation: Under the Offences Against the State Act 1939, 'scheduled offences' (listed in the First Schedule to the Act) are automatically tried in the SCC without the need for a DPP certificate in each individual case. Scheduled offences include offences under the Act itself (e.g., membership of an unlawful organisation under s.21, possession of firearms) and any offences added by Government order. For non-scheduled offences, the DPP must certify in each case that the ordinary courts are inadequate — this is the s.47(2) certificate procedure. The SCC's jurisdiction thus extends to both scheduled offences (automatically) and non-scheduled offences (by certificate).

questions.question: Following the Criminal Justice (Amendment) Act 2009, which extended the SCC's jurisdiction to certain organised crime offences, a challenge was brought arguing the extension was unconstitutional. The courts' response reflected:

options.A: That the 2009 extension was unconstitutional as it went beyond the terrorist threat that Art. 38.3 was designed to address
options.B: That Art. 38.3 is not confined to terrorism — it permits the Oireachtas to provide for special courts where ordinary courts are inadequate in the face of any serious threat to public peace and order, including organised crime, and the extension was constitutionally valid
options.C: That organised crime offences must always be tried by jury and cannot be scheduled
options.D: That the 2009 Act was invalid as it was not passed during a state of emergency

questions.explanation: Art. 38.3's reference to situations where the ordinary courts are 'inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice, and the preservation of public peace and order' is not textually confined to subversive/terrorist activities. The Oireachtas may extend the SCC's remit to other serious threats — including organised crime — where it determines that ordinary courts are inadequate. The Criminal Justice (Amendment) Act 2009 extended scheduled offences to certain organised crime provisions, and challenges to its constitutional validity have not succeeded. The adequacy determination is primarily for the Oireachtas, subject to limited judicial oversight.

questions.question: An accused successfully applies to the High Court by judicial review to have the DPP's certificate set aside on the ground that the DPP acted in bad faith. What is the consequence?

options.B: The case is remitted to the ordinary courts (Circuit Court or Central Criminal Court, as appropriate) for trial by jury
options.C: The SCC is required to discharge the accused from custody immediately
options.D: The DPP must retire — a bad faith finding disqualifies the DPP from office

questions.explanation: Where the High Court quashes the DPP's certificate on judicial review grounds (e.g., bad faith or improper motive), the constitutional basis for SCC jurisdiction in that case falls away. The consequence is that the accused must be tried before the appropriate ordinary court — the Circuit Court or Central Criminal Court, depending on the nature of the offence — with the full jury trial guarantee of Art. 38.5 restored. The DPP may have power to re-certify in good faith if the underlying basis for the certificate still exists, but this would itself be subject to challenge.
